coredata.dc:title: Aggregation-induced-emission red carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of norfloxacin and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Daohan
Liu, Lei
Li, Chunyan
coredata.dc:description: The detection of trace antibiotic residues holds significant importance because it’s related to food safety and human health. In this study, we developed a new high-yield red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with aggregation-induced emission properties for ratiometric sensing of norfloxacin. R-CDs were prepared in 30 min using an economical and efficient microwave-assisted method with tartaric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, achieving a high yield of 34.4 %. R-CDs showed concentration-dependent fluorescence and aggregation-induced-emission properties. A ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting the norfloxacin was developed. In the range of 0–40 μM, the intensity ratio of two emission peaks (I445 nm/I395 nm) towards norfloxacin show good linear relationship with its concentrations and a low detection limit was obtained (36.78 nM). In addition, complex patterns were developed for anti-counterfeiting based on different emission phenomenon at different concentrations. In summary, this study designed a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of norfloxacin, which greatly shortens the detection time and improves efficiency compared with high-performance liquid chromatography and other methods. The study will promote the application of carbon dots in anti-counterfeiting and other related fields, laying the foundation for the preparation of low-cost photosensitive anti-counterfeiting materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Quinolones
Anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of R-CDs: (a) TEM image and particle size distribution of R-CDs; (b) FTIR spectrum of R-CDs; (c) XPS spectrum of R-CDs; (d) C1s spectrum; (e) N1s spectrum; (f) O1s spectrum.

Optical properties of R-CDs: (a) UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs; (b) The emission of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–360 nm); (c) The emission of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (520–580 nm); (d) Light stability of R-CDs. Each sample was measured three times and error bars represent the standard deviation.

AIE properties of R-CDs: (a) Fluorescence of R-CDs solid under sunlight and 365 nm UV light; (b) Fluorescence of R-CDs in mixed solvents with different water proportions; (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs in mixed solvents with different water proportions; (d) Solutions of R-CDs at different concentrations; (e) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs at different concentrations.

Optimization of selectivity and detection conditions of R-CDs: (a) Solutions of R-CDs and R-CDs with different antibiotics under sunlight and UV light (365 nm); (b)Excitation-emission contour maps at different excitation wavelengths (ex = 300–600 nm); (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs with different antibiotics; (d) Anti-interference of other antibiotics on R-CDs-NOR detection system (blank: R-CDs-NOR solution); (e) Emission variation of the R-CDs-NOR detection system for 0–10 min; (f) pH optimization (in the range of pH 1–13) of R-CDs-NOR detection system. Each sample was measured three times and error bars represent the standard deviation.

Detection of NOR by R-CDs: (a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs towards NOR with different concentrations; (b) Linear range of I445nm/I395nm with NOR concentration (0–40 μM); (c) PS color discrimination system; (d) Line graph of R/B of R-CDs solution with NOR concentration (0–20 μM). Each sample was measured three times and error bars represent the standard deviation.
Application of R-CDs in anti-counterfeiting: (a) simple pattern; (b) complex pattern.

head.ce:title: Aggregation-induced-emission red carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of norfloxacin and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang 050018, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang 050018, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of trace antibiotic residues holds significant importance because it’s related to food safety and human health. In this study, we developed a new high-yield red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) with aggregation-induced emission properties for ratiometric sensing of norfloxacin. R-CDs were prepared in 30 min using an economical and efficient microwave-assisted method with tartaric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors, achieving a high yield of 34.4 %. R-CDs showed concentration-dependent fluorescence and aggregation-induced-emission properties. A ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting the norfloxacin was developed. In the range of 0–40 μM, the intensity ratio of two emission peaks (I445 nm/I395 nm) towards norfloxacin show good linear relationship with its concentrations and a low detection limit was obtained (36.78 nM). In addition, complex patterns were developed for anti-counterfeiting based on different emission phenomenon at different concentrations. In summary, this study designed a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of norfloxacin, which greatly shortens the detection time and improves efficiency compared with high-performance liquid chromatography and other methods. The study will promote the application of carbon dots in anti-counterfeiting and other related fields, laying the foundation for the preparation of low-cost photosensitive anti-counterfeiting materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the advent of antibiotics, they have been widely used in the medical field to treat infections caused by various bacteria. Among them, quinolones (QNs) are synthetic broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs [1], including ofloxacin, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, etc. By inhibiting the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) gyrase activity of bacteria, quinolones interfere with the DNA replication process, thereby achieving a good bactericidal effect [2,3]. Due to their advantages of economy and being less likely to develop cross-resistance, antibiotics are also widely used in farming to promote the growth of livestock and plants and prevent bacterial infections [4,5]. However, drug residues gradually accumulate over time [6], yielding unhealthy food products and causing pollution to the environment [7,8]. Long-term consumption of such food products containing quinolone residues can lead to various diseases and symptoms of digestive tract, central nervous system, and allergy [9]. In order to regulate the reasonable use of quinolones, many countries have formulated restrictions and regulations. In the announcement of the Ministry of Agriculture issued in 2019, China explicitly banned the use of four quinolones including ofloxacin in livestock. However, illegal and excess use of quinolones is still common due to high profit, it is necessary to develop a rapid, convenient and economical detection method for quinolones.

Currently, there are many detection methods for various quinolones including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–12], high-performance capillary electrophoresis (HPCE) [13–15], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [16–18], microbiological methods [19–21], immunoassay technology [22–24], etc. However, most of the above methods have problems such as expensive instrument requirements, complicated sample processing steps, and high detection limits. Fluorescence spectroscopy has been applied in recent years, especially fluorescence spectroscopy based on carbon dots (CDs) [25–29]. Due to advantages such as high sensitivity, convenient operation and implementable preparation [30–35], coupled with the unique and excellent fluorescence properties, CDs have become a new star in the field of drug analysis [36–42]. Zhang et al. [43] used hawthorn to prepare environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped CDs and applied it in the detection of chlortetracycline in pork based on fluorescence quenching phenomenon. The fluorescence of CDs synthesized by Tang et al. [44]can be effectively quenched by curcumin, and the detection limit of curcumin is as low as 29.8 nM. However, the simple quenching mode fluorescence sensing may be affected by experimental environment due to its high sensitivity. Comparatively, ratiometric fluorescent probes can solve this problem very well. Its advantage lies in its ability to effectively correct environmental interference, thereby reducing errors [45].

In this study, we synthesized red carbon dots with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property and established a ratiometric fluorescent probe for norfloxacin (NOR). As shown in Scheme 1 , crude carbon dots were prepared using an efficient microwave-assisted method using tartaric acid (TA) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as precursors. Afterwards, the crude sample undergo column chromatography separation to obtain pure R-CDs solid. R-CDs exhibit concentration-dependent fluorescence and show gradual enhancement when more poor solvent was added, verifying its AIE property. With 340 nm as the excitation wavelength, NOR can reduce the emission intensity of R-CDs at 395 nm and simutaneously a new peak (445 nm) appears. Based on this phenomenon, a ratiometric fluorescent probe for NOR was constructed with a good linear range 0–40 μM. At the same time, simple and advanced anti-counterfeiting application was achieved taking advantage of AIE effect of R-CDs and solid emission. Compared with HPLC and other methods, the new ratiometric fluorescent probe designed in this study greatly shortens the detection time and improves the efficiency. At the same time, the study also promotes the application of carbon dots in anti-counterfeiting and other related fields, laying the foundation for the preparation of low-cost photosensitive anti-counterfeiting materials.

Ofloxacin (OFL), levofloxacin (LEV), norfloxacin (NOR), ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CIP), gatifloxacin (GAT), metronidazole (MRN), ornidazole (ORN), tetracycline (TC), chloramphenicol (CHL), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were procured from Macklin. Tartaric acid (TA), anhydrous methanol, dichloromethane, and anhydrous formic acid were obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized water was used for all experiments, and all chemicals were of analytical grade. Milk and eggs were sourced from fresh food supermarkets in the vicinity of Hebei University of Science and Technology.

RE-2000B rotary evaporator (Shanghai Yarong Biochemical Instrument Factory); chromatography column (30 × 500 mm, Shanghai Xiamei Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.); column chromatography silica gel (300–400 mesh, Qingdao Ocean Chemical Factory); MD2 Opule microwave digestion instrument (Opule Technology Group Co., Ltd.); DZ-28C vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Test Instrument Co., Ltd.); F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lingguang Technology Co., Ltd.); TU-1900 dual-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV, Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.); JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan); Nicole iS 10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA); ThermoScientific NexsaX X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

0.75 g TA and 0.5 g OPD were placed in a beaker, then 50 mL of deionized water was added. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, and then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor for microwave digestion at 160 °C with a period of 30 min. The obtained product was filtered, and the obtained solid was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 1 h. The crude product was weighed to be 0.78 g, with a yield of 52 %. Then the crude product was separated by column chromatography, eluents were methanol: dichloromethane = 1:7 (volume ratio). The obtained liquid with red fluorescence was collected and rotary evaporated at 50 °C. 0.43 g R-CDs solid was collected, and the yield was 34.4 %.

The relative quantum yield was calculated by measuring the UV absorption of the sample using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. At the same time, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) can be used to comprehensively understand the molecular structure, functional group composition and elemental composition of the sample. The fluorescence spectrum of R-CDs was recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the optimal excitation conditions were screened out. In addition, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) provides us with morphological information of R-CDs, which is crucial for in-depth understanding of their microstructure and morphological characteristics.

Good solvent (formic acid) and poor solvent (water) were decided, and the proportions of water were adjusted to 0 %, 5 %, 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 % and 90 % respectively. R-CDs solid was dissolved in the above solvent systems to observe their color changes under UV light, then determine the optimal solvent system with the most enhanced fluorescence. In addition, the optimal solution system was used to prepare R-CDs solutions of different concentrations.

To construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe, we chose 340 nm as the excitation wavelength. Use OFL, LEV, NOR, GAT, CIP, MRN, ORN, TC, CHL, OTC, and CTC as the analytes to study the selectivity of R-CDs. Common ions such as Na+, NH4 +, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cl−, F−, Br−, SO4 2−, PO4 3− and NO3 – are used as interfering ions. First, the selectivity of R-CDs for antibiotics was investigated. 10 μM antibiotic solution and R-CDs solution were mixed and remained for 1 min, then fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was measured and fluorescence intensities at different positions were recorded, which were used to calculate fluorescence intensity ratio. Second, interference experiments were carried out. 0.5 mL NOR and 0.5 mL interfering ions were mixed with 4 mL of R-CDs, the fluorescence spectra were measured and fluorescence intensity ratio were calculated. Finally, sensitivity experiments were carried out. 0.5 mL of NOR solutions (0–40 μM) were mixed with 4.5 mL of R-CDs solution. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and concentration was established to calculate the detection limit according to LOD = 3σ/k.

The detection performance was tested in samples including milk and egg. 5 mL of anhydrous methanol was added into 50 mL of milk to precipitate the protein and fat, and centrifuge in a 3000 r/min centrifuge for 30 min. The supernatant was transferred to a clean volumetric flask for later use. Egg samples were prepared according to the method in the article of Sajwan et al. [46]. Mixture of milk and egg samples with R-CDs, respectively were prepared for fluorescence measurement at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The fluorescence spectra of R-CDs in milk and in egg were compared with R-CDs in H2O under the same conditions to exclude interference of milk and egg entity.
1–200 μg/mL R-CDs solution in 30 % formic acid was used to draw patterns on paper.

The morphology and particle size of R-CDs were observed using TEM. Fig. 1 a is the TEM image of R-CDs. It can be seen that R-CDs is well dispersed with uniform spherical morphology. The particle size distribution is in the range of 2.1–5.7 nm (Fig. 1a inset). FTIR spectrometer and XPS were used to comprehensively understand the composition of functional groups and elemental composition of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the broad peak from 3111.3 to 3500 cm−1 represents N–H or O–H [47]. 1727.4 cm−1 and 1592.9 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of CO and CC respectively [48]. 1398 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of C-N [49]. The sharp peaks at 1074.3 cm−1 confirmed the existence of C-O [50]. In the full spectrum of XPS in Fig. 1c, 284.88 eV, 400.76 eV and 532.29 eV represent the main peak of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively [51]. Among them, the proportions of C, N, and O elements are 56.8 %, 9.88 %, and 33.32 % respectively. The C1s spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. 1d) was fitted to obtain three peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.1 eV and 287.9 eV, which represent C–C/CC, C-N/C-O and CN/CO respectively[49,52]. Fig.1e shows the N1s spectrum, in which the presence of C-N = C is indicated at 399.1 eV [53], the peak at 399.6 eV indicates NC [54], and the peak at 400.7 eV proves that R-CDs also have an N–H structure [53]. In the subsequent O1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 1f, the two fitted peaks at 532.35 eV and 531.21 eV further confirmed the existence of C-O and CO [55].

The optical properties of R-CDs were studied in detail through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows the UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs. For the UV spectrum, the peaks at 270 nm and 289 nm represent the π → π* transition of aromatic CC in the carbon core. In particular, the absorption peaks at 566 nm and 615 nm represent n → π* transitions containing CO, CN, C-O and C-N bonds [56]. This obvious absorption feature provides important information about the molecular structure and energy band structure of R-CDs, laying the foundation for further understanding of their optical properties. In the fluorescence spectrum of Fig. 2a, it is observed that R-CDs exhibit dual emission at 633 nm and 667 nm when the excitation wavelength is 560 nm. This shows that R-CDs have two different red-fluorescence forms under specific excitation. We then examined the emission of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c, R-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-independent behavior with dual emission centers in the range of excitation wavelengths of 300–360 nm and 520–580 nm, indicating that R-CDs are likely to emit light in a molecular state [57,58]. This also supports the statement that carbon dots prepared from CA and small molecule amines mainly emit light in the molecular state [59]. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was calculated to be up to 40.45 % ensuring its application of R-CDs in sensors, anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

To study the photostability of R-CDs, we continuously exposed R-CDs solids to sunlight for 15 days. Weigh equal amounts of solids and dissolve them in a mixed solvent of formic acid and water (v/v = 3:7) after 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 and 15 days of irradiation then record the fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2d, during the test process, the fluorescence intensity at different emission positions decreased slightly, but the change was not significant, which proved that R-CDs have good photostability and in respect of sample storage. In addition, we also examined the thermal stability and salt stability of R-CDs. The results show that with changes in temperature and salt concentration, the fluorescence intensity at different emission positions of R-CDs does not change significantly (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2).

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the solid R-CDs appear bright red under 365 nm UV light, suggesting the AIE property of R-CDs. Experiments were done to verify the AIE property. First, the fluorescence of R-CDs in mixed solvents (formic acid as good solvent, water as poor solvent) with different volume ratios were examined. As shown in Fig. 3b, as the proportion of water gradually increases, the R-CDs solution color under UV light (365 nm) change from lilac to bright red. Fig. 3c shows the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs in mixed solvents. It is worth noting that when the proportion of water is 70 %, the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum value, then the fluorescence intensity decreases when water ratio is 90 %. We speculate that R-CDs form larger aggregates in the mixed solvent (water ratio 90 %), resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to the π-π stacking effect [60]. Alternatively, this phenomenon may also be due to size differences in the aggregates [61]. At the same time, fluorescence spectra also gradually red-shifted as the ratio of water increased (Fig. 3c). We observed the UV absorption of R-CDs with different solvent fractions (water: formic acid) and found that as the water volume fraction gradually increased, the UV absorption of R-CDs showed a blue shift (Fig. S3). We speculate that the increase in poor solvents causes the aggregation of R-CDs to be more inclined to H-aggregates [62]. However, when the concentration gradually increases, the absorbance of UV absorption of R-CDs gradually increases, and the position of the absorption peak almost remains unchanged (Fig. S4). Maybe the aggregation forms in solvent fractions and concentration conditions are different. We further examined the fluorescence of R-CDs solutions with different concentrations and recorded the corresponding fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. 3e, as the concentration of R-CDs gradually increased from 0.1 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL, it was observed that the fluorescence of the solutions gradually enhanced, and at the same time, the emission peak at 623 nm gradually red-shifted to 645 nm. As explained previously, the increase in molecular aggregation leads to an increase in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) frequency, causing a red shift in the fluorescence spectrum at high concentrations [63,64]. The above results show the solvent and concentration-dependent fluorescence properties of R-CDs, provides experimental basis for practical applications.

The selectivity of R-CDs was investigated over 11 antibiotics. As shown in Fig. 4 a, OFL, LEV, CIP, GAT and NOR caused obvious color change. Fig. 4b is the contour plot of the excitation-emission spectrum of R-CDs showing two excitation centers 340 nm and 560 nm, which means that R-CDs can be excited by two different excitation sources, resulting in different emissions sites (395 nm and 633 nm, 667 nm). To construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe, we measured the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs-antibiotics at different excitations. As shown in Fig. S5, when the excitation wavelength is 560 nm, the two emission peaks at 633 nm and 667 nm do not change significantly. When 340 nm is used as the excitation, the addition of OFL, LEV, GAT and NOR produce new emission peaks at 506 nm, 506 nm, 501 nm and 445 nm, respectively, while the emission peak at 395 nm decreases, as shown in Fig. 4c. We note that OFL, LEV and GAT induce similar new emissions around 500 nm while NOR induce new emission at 445 nm, which is significantly different from those of other four. Therefore, R-CDs achieved the selective detection of NOR by the new peak (445 nm). Since quinolones contain conjugated skeletons, we also tested the fluorescence spectrum of NOR under 340 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence emission of 100 μM NOR solution is located at 439 nm.

Common interfering ions have no interference on the detection (Fig. S7). In addition, we also examined the interference of various antibiotics on the R-CDs-NOR detection system. As shown in Fig. 4d, the impact of 10 common antibiotics, including but not limited to OTC, on the I445nm/I395nm ratio is not significant. The above results show that in this detection system, these common ions and antibiotics have no obvious interference effect on the NOR detection results. In order to optimize the detection effect, we also examined the response time and pH, as shown in Fig. 4e, NOR achieve detetion within 1 min.We examined the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs-NOR in the pH range of 1–13, as shown in Fig. 4f, and determined pH 9 as the optimal pH condition.

In order to verify the sensitivity of R-CDs for NOR, we mixed R-CDs with NOR solutions with gradient concentration (0–40 μM). A linear relationship was established between I445nm/I395nm and NOR concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5 a, as the concentration of NOR solution increases, the emission peak of R-CDs at 395 nm gradually decreases, and the emission peak at 445 nm gradually increases. The color of the system gradually changes from red to violet. In Fig. 5b, the NOR concentration has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–20 μM, and the detection limit is 36.78 nM. It can be seen that the method we constructed can achieve sensitive detection for NOR. Due to the different fluorescence responses of several antibiotics to R-CDs, the detection mechanism is quite complex and requires further research. Since NOR can significantly change the fluorescence color of R-CDs, we develop the photoshop (PS) color discrimination system, as shown in Fig. 5c. We generate RGB color code using PS software for R-CDs solutions with different concentrations of NOR. Then a linear relationship between R/B and NOR concentration was established, which shows that the quantitative detection of NOR can be achieved through the PS color discrimination system (Fig. 5d).

We examined the feasibility of the detection system in milk and eggs samples. The results are shown in Table S1. The recovery rates of NOR in actual samples were all between 96.82 %−104.51 %, and the relative standard deviation range was 1.42 %−3.28 %. This further proves that the method has high accuracy and is suitable for detection of actual samples. In addition, we also listed the detection of NOR by some different carbon dots and different methods (Table S2). By comparing with previous research work, we conclude that the R-CDs prepared in this paper have relatively superior sensing performance to detect NOR. This superior performance may be related to the purification method adopted during the preparation process. We speculate that carbon dots with high purity may have greater advantages in sensing applications.

We used R-CDs solutions of different concentrations as ink to draw the patterns on filter paper and observed its fluorescence under daylight and 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. 6 a, as the concentration of R-CDs solution decreases, the color of the pattern on the filter paper gradually become lighter. The gradient color provides possibilities for the design of more complex anti-counterfeiting patterns. As shown in Fig. 6b, multi level pattern are observed under 365 nm UV light, providing complex anti-counterfeiting pattern using one material.

In summary, this study used TA and OPD as raw materials to synthesize R-CDs by a simple and environmentally friendly microwave-assisted method in high yield of 34.4 %. We successfully developed a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of NOR with low detection limit of 36.78 nM. The feasibility of the detection was verified in real samples of milk and egg. It provides an effective method for detection of NOR in water and animal foods. In addition, with have excellent aggregation-induced luminescence properties, R-CDs can be applied in simple and complex anti-counterfeiting. This further enriches its application. Overall, this study not only provides a simple and efficient synthesis of high-performance R-CDs with high yield, but also developed a ratiometric fluorescent probe with multiple application potential.

Daohan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lei Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chunyan Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: ei Liu reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China, Department of Chemical Sciences, Division of Environmental Chemistry. Lei Liu reports financial support was provided by Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2020208043). Lei Liu reports financial support was provided by Hebei innovation center of pharmaceutical and chemical engineering (225676121H). If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21102032), Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2020208043), Hebei innovation center of pharmaceutical and chemical engineering (225676121H).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124186.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical importance and epidemiology of quinolone resistance
Infect Chemother.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.S. Kim, D.C. Hooper, Clinical Importance and Epidemiology of Quinolone Resistance, Infect Chemother, 46 (2014) 226-238, Doi: 10.3947/ic.2014.46.4.226

Curr. Top. Med. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanism of quinolone action and resistance
Biochemistry

J. Chromatogr. A

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Restricted access media-imprinted nanomaterials based on a metal–organic framework for highly selective extraction of fluoroquinolones in milk and river water
J. Chromatogr. A
